0.event: Up in flames. A spark in the sawdust and the whole camp burns — stacked timber, tools, shelters, all of it. The foreman is caught in the blaze, dead or gravely maimed (your call). The camp must be rebuilt from nothing.

1.event: Widowmaker. A half-felled tree drops without warning. Workers are badly hurt and the crew refuses to return to that stand until something is done about it.

2.event: Rot in the stack. The season's cut is discovered riddled with rot — or someone has been quietly skimming logs. Either way the stockpile is worthless and the foreman's standing takes a visible hit.

3.event: Bad blood. The two-man saw snaps mid-cut and a feud erupts over whose fault it was. Crews split into camps and work halts for days.

4.event: Foul weather. Storms turn the skid trails to mud. Time lost, little gained.

5.event: Green hand. A new axeman's mistake ruins a full day's cut. Lesson learned the hard way.

6.event: Hard timber. Every tree this season is knotted and stubborn. The logs fight back.

7.event: Lost load. A sledge tips on the slope and half a haul ends up at the bottom of a ravine.

8.event: Just work. Axes, sawdust, and routine. Nothing to report.

9.event: A good dog. A stray mutt adopts the crew and starts guarding the log pile at night. Beloved, useless, and now the camp's unofficial mascot.

10.event: Fair trade. A passing traveler swaps a decent whetstone for a few boards. The axes bite a little truer for a while.

11.event: Old song. An elder worker teaches the crew a felling-song to time their strokes. Nothing mechanical — but the camp starts to feel like theirs.

12.event: Clean grain. A run of straight, true timber comes down. Whoever builds with this will be pleased.

13.event: The clearing. Felling opens a fine sheltered clearing — a natural spot for a future building, camp, or field.

14.event: The squirrel. A bold squirrel keeps raiding the crew's lunches, and outwitting it becomes the week's great sport. No mechanical effect — just a warm memory.

15.event: Honest surplus. A tidy extra cord of firewood turns up. Small comfort against the coming cold.

16.event: Solid week. The camp hums. Everyone comes home tired and a little proud.

17.event: Fine boards. A batch of exceptional, furniture-grade planks comes off the saw. Word will travel if there's anyone out there to hear it.

18.event: Rescued traveller. Loggers find someone lost or hurt in the deep woods and carry them home. A potential recruit, contact, or quest hook wanders into your lap.

19.effect: Permanent minor efficiency for this building.
19.event: The right cut. The foreman works out a faster way to drop and skid timber with the tools at hand. A small standing bonus from now on.

20.event: Rare wood. The crew fells a stand of something special — ironwood, silverbark, heartoak. Enough for one exceptional commissioned piece.

21.event: The far smoke. From a high stand, loggers spot distant chimney smoke — the first hard proof that someone else lives in this world. A direction to explore.

22.event: The old grove. Cutting deep, the crew uncovers the edge of an ancient grove — ruins, a shrine, or something older tangled in the roots. A full adventure hook in a nearby hex.

23.event: Master's eye. A wandering woodwright, drawn by the sound of good work, offers to stay a season. Superior craft for a while — and a permanent recruit if courted well.

24.event: The great tree. The crew brings down a colossal ancient trunk — enough single-piece timber to raise a hall, a bridge, or a boat. A landmark project becomes possible.

25.effect: Standing morale/social benefit to the settlement.
25.event: The long fire. The camp's evening fire has quietly become where the whole settlement gathers to talk, sing, and settle disputes. It now functions as a hub.

26.event: Buried cache. Splitting an old stump, workers find a hidden cache — coin, a map, or a relic stashed by hands generations gone. Treasure, with a mystery attached.

27.effect: Lasting boost to the value of this settlement's timber.
27.event: Named work. Something raised from this camp's timber is so fine that travelers begin to speak of it. The settlement's wood earns a name.

28.event: The living wood. A felled tree proves to be more than timber — a dryad's home, a bound spirit, a seed of something ancient. Handled well, a powerful guardian for the town. Handled poorly, a reckoning.

29.event: Heartwood beyond price. The crew opens a log to find heartwood that shimmers like oil on water — wood that takes enchantment the way cloth takes dye. Enough for one wonder.

30.event: The sword in the wood. The saw bites into a great log and rings against steel. Buried in the heartwood, whole and gleaming and unmistakably magical, is a sword — waiting. No one knows how it got there. A story told for years.

0.event: The red season. Something big and wrong has moved into the hunting grounds. It has taken hunters, scattered the game, and found the smokehouse — every stored ration is destroyed or fouled, and the foreman is among the missing (dead or worse, your call). Until it is dealt with, the woods feed no one.

1.event: Mauled. A hunt goes wrong — a boar, a bear, a fall. Hunters come home carried, and the rest are shaken.

2.event: Spoiled stores. The smokehouse fails — damp, flies, or carelessness — and a season of meat is lost. Someone should have caught it, and everyone knows who.

3.event: The empty woods. The game has simply gone. Trails cold, snares untouched. Something has spooked it — but what?

4.event: Hard rain. A week of downpour washes out every trail and scent. Lean pickings.

5.event: Snapped string. Bowstrings, snares, and nets all seem to fail at once. The gear needs a full overhaul.

6.event: Lean game. What the hunters bring back is skin and bone. The land is stingier than hoped this season.

7.event: The one that got away. A magnificent stag shakes off a sure arrow and vanishes. Pride wounded, larder light.

8.event: Just hunting. Long walks, small game, quiet woods. Nothing to report.

9.event: The raven. A one-eyed raven starts following the hunting parties, croaking at nothing. The hunters have started leaving it scraps, just in case.

10.event: Berry ground. A forager stumbles on a reliable patch of berries and roots. A modest, steady supplement.

11.event: First blood. The settlement's youngest hunter takes their first game and can't stop grinning. The lodge feels a little more like a family.

12.event: Good tracks. The hunters map the local game trails properly at last. The woods make a little more sense now.

13.event: The fish run. A nearby stream turns out to run thick with fish in season. A new food source worth remembering.

14.event: The staring contest. A hunter and a fox sat looking at each other across a clearing for a full minute before both walked away. No effect — but nobody at the fire tells it the same way twice.

15.event: Full smokehouse. An honest surplus of cured meat goes into store. Winter looks a little less frightening.

16.event: Fat season. The game is plentiful and the hunters are sharp. The whole settlement eats well.

17.event: Fine pelts. A run of flawless furs comes in — warm, beautiful, and valuable to anyone worth trading with.

18.event: The stranger at the snare. Hunters find someone in the deep woods — lost, hurt, or hiding. A potential recruit, contact, or quest hook, carried home over a shoulder.

19.effect: Permanent minor efficiency for this building.
19.event: The quiet way. The foreman works out how to read this land — wind, water, habit. Hunts run shorter and come home heavier from now on.

20.event: The great beast. The hunters bring down a truly enormous animal. Meat for weeks, a trophy for the hall, and a story everyone will claim to have been there for.

21.event: Foreign arrow. A hunter cuts a broken arrow from a carcass — fletched in a style nobody recognizes. Someone else hunts these woods. A direction to explore.

22.event: The bone hollow. Trailing wounded game, hunters find a hollow floored with old bones and older carvings. Something was worshipped here once. A full adventure hook in a nearby hex.

23.event: The old tracker. A weathered hunter walks out of the treeline, sizes up the lodge, and offers to teach — for a place at the fire. A master for a season, a recruit if courted well.

24.event: The herd. Scouts find the migration route of a vast herd passing near the settlement each year. Managed wisely, it could feed the town indefinitely — a landmark opportunity.

25.effect: Standing morale/social benefit to the settlement.
25.event: The feast. The lodge hosts a hunt-feast that becomes, on the spot, the settlement's first true tradition. It now anchors the town's calendar and spirit.

26.event: The swallowed ring. Dressing a great fish or beast, a hunter finds a ring, key, or token in its gut — old, fine, and clearly belonging to someone. Treasure, with a mystery attached.

27.effect: Lasting boost to the value of this settlement's furs and game.
27.event: The named hunt. Word of a legendary local beast — and the lodge that dared it — begins to spread. Hunters and traders will come asking.

28.event: The white stag. A stag of impossible size and pale as frost lets itself be seen — and waits, as if inviting a follow. Where it leads is yours to decide. Handled with respect, a blessing on these woods. Hunted, a curse.

29.event: The horn of plenty. In a beast's den the hunters find an old carved horn that, once a season, fills with whatever a hungry hand needs most. A relic of the wild — and the wild may want it back.

30.event: The wolf that speaks. A great wolf walks into the firelight, sits, and speaks — a greeting, a warning, or a bargain, in a voice like winter. It knows this world. It is willing to talk. A story told for years.

0.event: Collapse in the night. The bunkhouse comes down while people sleep. There are deaths — your call how many and who — and the foreman is pulled from the wreckage broken, if at all. Every scrap of accumulated comfort and trust is gone; the survivors sleep under the sky and remember why they're afraid of it.

1.event: The roof gives. A whole section of roof caves in a storm. Soaked bedding, ruined belongings, and everyone crammed into what's left.

2.event: Shoddy work exposed. A wall bows, a beam cracks, and it becomes plain the work was never sound. The settlers' faith in whoever built this takes a hard, public knock.

3.event: The falling-out. A petty theft or a snoring feud boils over into a brawl that splits the bunkhouse into factions. The air is poison for days.

4.event: Vermin. Rats — or worse — in the walls. Nobody sleeps well and nothing edible is safe.

5.event: The leak. A drip over someone's bunk that no amount of patching seems to find. Small, maddening, endless.

6.event: Cold snap. Bitter nights find every gap in the walls. Everyone wakes tired and short-tempered.

7.event: Crowding. Tempers fray over space, bunks, and whose boots those are. Nothing breaks, but nothing rests either.

8.event: Just sleep. Creaking boards, shared warmth, ordinary nights. Nothing to report.

9.event: The house mouse. A single, oddly polite mouse takes up residence and is — against all reason — adopted rather than evicted. It has a name now.

10.event: The mended quilt. Someone stitches the settlement's spare rags into one enormous shared quilt. Warmth, and something like home.

11.event: Ghost stories. Someone starts telling stories after the candles go out, and now it's a nightly ritual. The dark feels smaller.

12.event: Proper bunks. Real bedframes at last, off the cold ground. Sleep gets deeper and backs stop aching.

13.event: The hearth draws. The fireplace finally draws properly, and the bunkhouse is warm through for the first time. People linger in the evenings now.

14.event: The snore chorus. Three settlers snore in accidental three-part harmony, and someone stays up half the night giggling about it. No effect — but it becomes the settlement's first inside joke.

15.event: Room to breathe. A clever rearrangement finds space nobody thought was there. The crowding eases and so do tempers.

16.event: Rested. A stretch of genuinely good nights. The whole settlement works better for it and knows exactly why.

17.event: The common table. Someone builds a long table, and eating together becomes the rule instead of the exception. The settlement starts talking like one household.

18.event: The knock at the door. A traveler, refugee, or wanderer arrives at the bunkhouse door asking shelter. A potential recruit, contact, or quest hook, delivered to the doorstep.

19.effect: Permanent minor efficiency for this building.
19.event: Sound at last. The foreman finds and fixes the deep flaws in the structure — properly, this time. The bunkhouse holds heat and holds together from now on.

20.event: The wedding. Two settlers announce they mean to marry — the settlement's first. Preparation, celebration, and the sudden feeling that people are building lives here, not just shelters.

21.event: Word of home. Talk in the bunks turns to the old world, and settlers pool what they know — names, routes, reasons for leaving. A clearer picture of where everyone came from, and threads worth pulling.

22.event: What's under the floor. Levelling the floor, workers strike worked stone — a buried foundation far older than it should be. Someone built here before. A full adventure hook, directly underfoot.

23.event: The healer's hands. One of the settlers turns out to have real skill with medicine or midwifery they'd never mentioned. A vital specialist, discovered sleeping ten feet away all along.

24.event: The first child. A baby is born in the bunkhouse — the first of the new world. Everything about the settlement suddenly means more, and everyone knows it.

25.effect: Standing morale/social benefit to the settlement.
25.event: The hall. Without anyone deciding it, the bunkhouse has become the place where the settlement gathers, argues, votes, and celebrates. It now functions as the town's heart.

26.event: The lining of the coat. Patching an old coat or bedroll, someone finds what was sewn inside it — coin, a letter, a small treasure smuggled from the old world. Wealth, with a story attached.

27.effect: Lasting boost to the settlement's ability to attract and keep settlers.
27.event: The open door. The bunkhouse's warmth becomes its reputation. Travelers speak of it, and people weighing where to build a life now think of here first.

28.event: The hearth spirit. Something small and old moves into the chimney — boots mended by morning, bread that never burns, a presence that likes being lied about. Kept fed and respected, the bunkhouse is quietly blessed. Insulted, it will sulk — or worse.

29.event: The shared dream. On the same night, every sleeper in the bunkhouse has the same dream — a place, a face, a warning or a promise somewhere out in the hexes. Whatever it is, it knows they're here.

30.event: The door that wasn't there. Morning light finds a door in the bunkhouse wall that no one built, fine-grained and warm to the touch. It opens somewhere else. It is, unmistakably, an invitation. A story told for years.

0.event: The night it fell. The tower burns or falls in a coordinated night attack — and it was no accident. The watch is slaughtered or scattered, the foreman among the lost (dead or taken, your call), and every defensive preparation is undone. Whoever did this knows exactly how the settlement defends itself now.

1.event: Breach. Raiders, beasts, or worse slip past the watch in the dark. People are hurt, things are taken, and everyone knows the tower failed.

2.event: Asleep at the post. A sentry is found sleeping — or missing — and the gap went unnoticed for hours. Discipline cracks and the foreman wears the blame.

3.event: False alarm. A panicked midnight alarm empties every bed for nothing. Twice. Trust in the bell itself begins to rot.

4.event: Fog. Days of grey soup. The watch sees nothing, which is exactly what worries them.

5.event: Thin ranks. Sickness and other duties leave the roster full of holes. The watch runs ragged covering shifts.

6.event: Prowlers. Something circles the settlement at night — tracks in the morning, eyes at the treeline. It takes nothing. Yet.

7.event: The rotten rung. A ladder rung gives and a sentry falls. Nothing broken but a wrist and everyone's confidence in the tower.

8.event: Just watching. Long cold shifts, empty horizon. Nothing to report — which is the job going well.

9.event: The owl. An owl takes up residence in the tower's eaves and glares at every sentry as if grading them. The watch has started calling it 'the Sergeant.'

10.event: The whittled watch. A bored sentry whittles a full set of little figures — one for every settler. They stand along the tower rail now, watching too.

11.event: The dawn shift. Watching the sun rise over an untouched world, night after night, starts to feel less like duty and more like a privilege. Sentries have begun requesting the dawn shift.

12.event: Sightlines. The watch clears brush and marks ranges around the settlement. Anything approaching now does so in the open.

13.event: The signal code. The watch works out a simple system of horn and lantern signals. The settlement can now speak across distance.

14.event: The deer alarm. The entire settlement is scrambled at midnight by the most dangerous-sounding deer in creation. No effect — but 'remember the deer' now defuses any argument in town.

15.event: Drilled. The foreman runs the settlers through their first proper alarm drill and it goes — to everyone's surprise — well. People know where to run now.

16.event: A quiet strength. The watch is sharp, the rotations clean, and the settlement sleeps easier for knowing it.

17.event: Turned away. The watch spots trouble early — raiders, a beast, a bad-intentioned band — and sees it off before it becomes a fight. Deterrence, proven.

18.event: Halt, who goes there. The watch intercepts a stranger approaching the settlement — openly or otherwise. A potential recruit, contact, or quest hook, caught at the perimeter.

19.effect: Permanent minor efficiency for this building.
19.event: Soldier's habits. The foreman quietly rebuilds the watch on real military lines — rotations, signals, discipline that sticks. The tower simply works from now on. Some settlers wonder aloud where he learned all this.

20.event: The captured scout. The watch takes a prisoner — a scout, spy, or straggler from some other power in this world. What they know, and what to do with them, is now the settlement's problem and prize.

21.event: Lights on the ridge. Night watch spots distant fires or signals answering across the far hills — organized, deliberate, and not aimed at you. Yet. Someone out there has watchtowers too.

22.event: The old battlefield. Scouting a new watch-line, the sentries find rusted weapons, old bones, and the unmistakable shape of an ancient battle. Who fought here, and over what? A full adventure hook in a nearby hex.

23.event: The veteran. A scarred traveler watches the sentries drill, corrects one's stance without asking, and offers to stay on. A soldier of real experience — and perhaps someone who recognizes the foreman.

24.event: The high tower. The watch identifies a commanding height — a crag, a ruin, a great tree — from which half the region could be watched. Claiming and building it would be a landmark project.

25.effect: Standing benefit: the settlement is markedly harder to surprise.
25.event: The beacon line. Outlying camps and the tower link into a true beacon network. From this day, the settlement sees trouble coming from miles out.

26.event: The officer's strongbox. Beneath the tower's new footing, diggers strike an old strongbox — pay chest, dispatches, or a sealed order from some long-dead army. Treasure, with a mystery attached.

27.effect: Lasting boost to the settlement's reputation for safety.
27.event: The safe road. Word spreads that the land under this tower's eye is safe to walk. Travelers begin routing their journeys through the settlement on purpose.

28.event: The unsleeping flame. Lit in an old brazier found in the earth, the tower's watchfire stops needing fuel — and stops going out. In its light, sentries see through fog, dark, and lies. Something is honoring the watch. Something may one day ask why.

29.event: The horn of the old war. The watch unearths a great horn of bone and bronze. Sounded, it is heard for impossible miles — and things older than the settlement stir in their sleep when it blows. A mighty alarm, and a question: who else remembers its call?

30.event: The knight on the road. At dawn the watch reports a single armored figure walking the road — armor a century out of fashion, banner of no known land, asking by name to speak with whoever leads here. It has been walking a very long time to find this place. A story told for years.

0.event: The empty shelves. Fire, flood, or a silent, systematic theft — the storehouse is stripped or destroyed, and every stockpiled resource the settlement holds is gone in a single night. The foreman is lost to it (dead, ruined, or vanished with the goods — your call). Winter arithmetic starts over at zero.

1.event: The flood line. Water gets in — a burst bank, a driving storm — and stands a foot deep among the stores. Days of salvage, much of it futile.

2.event: The false count. The ledgers say one thing; the shelves say another. Spoilage, theft, or sheer sloppy counting — whichever it is, the shortfall is real and the foreman's book is no longer trusted.

3.event: The hoarding panic. A rumor of shortage sends settlers quietly squirreling away supplies. The stores scatter into a hundred hiding places and the settlement briefly stops trusting itself.

4.event: Damp. Mold finds the sacks in the corner. A portion of the stores is lost to grey fuzz and regret.

5.event: Weevils. Something small and hungry got into the grain first. The pickiest eaters in the settlement learn to stop looking closely.

6.event: The mislaid crate. An entire crate of something important simply cannot be found. It will turn up in the spring, somewhere obvious, and no one will admit fault.

7.event: Short weights. Careless portioning has been quietly over-issuing for weeks. The books get balanced the hard way: thinner rations.

8.event: Just stores. Counting, stacking, turning the grain. Nothing to report, which is how a storehouse should be.

9.event: The store cat. A lean cat appears, audits the storehouse for mice, and hires itself. The foreman has begun entering it in the ledger as 'staff.'

10.event: The label system. Someone finally paints labels on every bin and barrel. Ten minutes of any search becomes ten seconds.

11.event: The tally songs. The counting work gets set to little rhymes to keep the numbers straight, and now the whole settlement hums inventory. Oddly companionable.

12.event: Dry and true. New shelving keeps everything off the ground and the damp out. Losses to spoilage drop noticeably.

13.event: The salvage. A careful re-sort of the settlement's baggage turns up forgotten useful things — tools, cloth, seed — packed in the old world and never unpacked.

14.event: The pickle incident. A mislabeled barrel leads to the most memorable stew the settlement has ever eaten. No effect — but it is never, ever forgotten.

15.event: Rationed right. The foreman's careful arithmetic stretches the stores further than anyone thought possible. Quiet, unglamorous, lifesaving work.

16.event: Well kept. The stores are dry, counted, and honest. The settlement plans its future on numbers it can trust.

17.event: Preserved. Someone masters a proper preserving method — salting, smoking, cellaring — and food that used to rot now keeps. The larder deepens.

18.event: The peddler. A lone trader with a laden mule finds the settlement and asks to do business. Goods, news of this world, and a potential recurring contact — the first taste of trade.

19.effect: Permanent minor efficiency for this building.
19.event: The steward's system. The foreman institutes a proper stores discipline — rotation, inspection, the whole quiet machinery of a well-run house. Waste falls, permanently.

20.event: The root cellar. Diggers open a deep, cold, dry cellar beneath the storehouse — natural or newly cut. Storage capacity leaps, and some things can now be kept that never could before.

21.event: The surplus. For the first time, the settlement holds genuinely more than it needs. The question — hoard it, invest it, or trade it — is suddenly real, and everyone has an opinion.

22.event: The sealed jars. Deep in the settlement's baggage sits a crate no one remembers packing: sealed jars, old wax, a script no settler reads. Opening them is a decision. A full adventure hook, shelved and waiting.

23.event: The quartermaster. A traveler with the unmistakable bearing of someone who has provisioned armies or great houses offers their services. A master of logistics — and a recruit, if courted well.

24.event: The granary. Stores, systems, and surplus align: the settlement can now build a true granary — a year's insurance against famine. A landmark project becomes possible.

25.effect: Standing benefit to settlement stability and trust.
25.event: The common store. The storehouse becomes the settlement's beating administrative heart — where shares are issued, disputes over goods settled, and the town's fairness made visible. Trust compounds like interest.

26.event: The double floor. Repairs reveal the storehouse floor is doubled — and the gap between is not empty. Coin, contraband, or something stranger, hidden by hands unknown. Treasure, with a mystery attached.

27.effect: Lasting boost to the settlement's trade capacity.
27.event: The full manifest. The foreman completes a true accounting of everything the settlement owns, produces, and needs. For the first time, the town knows exactly what it can offer the world — trade, at scale, becomes possible.

28.event: The honest scales. Among the salvage stands a set of old bronze scales that will not balance for a lie — false weights, false words, false hearts, all betrayed by the tilt of a pan. Justice, in instrument form. Word of them will travel.

29.event: The unspent coin. A coin turns up in the counting that is always in the purse the morning after it's spent. Small magic, enormous implications — and somewhere, presumably, a mint that wants it back.

30.event: The bottomless barrel. An unremarkable barrel in the corner is found to hold, each dawn, exactly what was drawn from it the day before. Grain, water, nails — it gives back whatever it held. No one knows which of the old world's gods to thank. A story told for years.
